concerns.text: The validation design contains a circular dependency. The 'sparsity level' (predictor) is defined as a percentage of the *same* dataset used to generate the 'ground truth' (target). In standard learning curve analysis, the 'truth' is the performance on a held-out, fixed test set. Here, FR-003 creates 7 subsets of the *entire* dataset, and SC-001 implies measuring error against a 'held-out test set' derived from these same subsets. If the test set shrinks as the training set shrinks (or if the 'full dataset' is the only source), the variance of the error metric is confounded with the sample size of the test set itself, not just the training density. The spec fails to explicitly mandate a FIXED, large, independent test set (e.g., 20% of the full data) that remains constant across all 7 training sparsity levels. Without this, the 'performance degradation' is a trivial mathematical artifact of smaller test sets having higher variance, not a genuine measure of model sparsity impact.

The stratification strategy for 'chemical space' is undefined and likely methodologically unsound for this field. FR-003 requires 'stratified random sampling... preserving chemical space distribution.' In materials science, 'chemical space' is high-dimensional (composition, structure, symmetry). Simple stratification on a single scalar (e.g., formation energy) does not preserve the *coverage* of chemical space. If the 5% subset is stratified only by energy, it may miss entire classes of elements or crystal structures present in the 100% set, causing the model to fail due to *domain shift* (extrapolation) rather than *sparsity* (interpolation density). The spec must define the stratification variable (e.g., clustering in composition space) or explicitly state that the goal is to measure the combined effect of sparsity and coverage loss, otherwise the results will be uninterpretable.

The statistical test (ANOVA) assumes independence of observations. However, the 7 sparsity levels are nested (the 5% set is a subset of the 10% set, etc., if using cumulative sampling, or highly correlated if using independent seeds). If the same random seed is used to generate nested subsets, the error metrics are not independent. If independent seeds are used, the variance in performance is driven by both the subset size and the specific random draw. The spec does not specify whether to use nested subsets (requiring repeated measures ANOVA or mixed-effects models) or independent subsets (requiring multiple seeds per level to estimate variance). A standard one-way ANOVA on single-run metrics from nested or highly correlated subsets violates the independence assumption, rendering the p-values invalid.
